summary: Joe Reis argues that traditional data management's pursuit of perfect data has consistently failed and is fundamentally unscalable given the explosion of data types and volume. He proposes that AI-driven data management, using LLM agents to evaluate and curate datasets, represents the field's best chance at finally achieving meaningful results.
key_points: Perfect data is a myth that data management has futilely chased for decades
Data complexity has grown beyond human capacity to manage manually
AI agents can reduce data curation from tens of thousands of hours to hundreds
The goal should be 'useful' datasets, not 'perfect' ones
money_quotes: The track record of data management's pursuit of perfection is beyond poor.
Perfect datasets very rarely exist. I'm not even sure what a perfect dataset looks like, but I do know what useful datasets look like.
Data management's ideal of 'grown-ups in the room' won't work anymore (it barely worked in the past). Unscalable problems for humans are perfect use cases for machine learning and AI.
Chain together various purpose-built data management AI agents, and you've got an army of data management 'professionals' working tirelessly on managing all of your data assets.
key_insight: Data management needs to abandon its perfectionist mindset and embrace AI agents as the only scalable solution for managing today's data explosion.
